one_line_takeaway: A single-author engineering framework and small proof-of-concept showing a digit-level Siamese Transformer avoids the float64 'Float Wall' that collapses LightGBM-based models on out-of-distribution numerical/physical extrapolation, but the paper's own largest-scale claims (30B samples, cosmic-scale NumberNet accuracy, constant rediscovery via PySR) are described as engineering infrastructure or future/ongoing work rather than results demonstrated in this document -- and it is not an AI-security paper.
key_finding: On the small-scale demonstration, the two float64-based LightGBM models (A and B) degrade as test magnitudes approach the Float Wall (~10^15) and collapse to 50.00% accuracy (chance level) beyond ~10^20, because decision-tree splits are axis-aligned thresholds that cannot extrapolate past their training range and because float64 cannot even represent the distinction between c and c+1 once magnitudes exceed ~10^16 (a formal point the paper backs with a dedicated script, prove_float_collapse.py). By contrast, the digit-tokenized NumberNet, because it operates on LSB-aligned digit sequences rather than fixed-precision floats, is argued to be structurally immune to this failure mode at arbitrary scale (up to 10^50 is claimed). This claim is architectural/qualitative, not a measured accuracy figure at 10^50 for NumberNet -- the paper reports the collapse numbers for Models A/B (50.00% baseline) but does not report a corresponding quantitative accuracy number for Model C at cosmic scale in the extracted text; its success is described but not tabulated alongside a metric. The overarching conclusion the author draws is that architecture (digit-level, magnitude-invariant tokenization), not data volume, determines whether a model can generalize to unseen numerical scales.
core_contribution: A self-published, single-author engineering framework ("Index-PT-Engine") for testing whether ML models can learn exact numerical/physical invariants rather than statistical approximations. The paper's own novel components are: (1) a digit-level Siamese Transformer, 'NumberNet' (a.k.a. NumberEncoder/LNNet_P), which tokenizes each numeric input variable as a separate ASCII digit sequence (LSB-first), encodes each independently through a shared Transformer encoder, pools via learned attention, and concatenates the resulting per-variable latent vectors into an interaction MLP; (2) a three-layer data-integrity engineering stack (write-time SHA-256 hashes embedded in Parquet schema metadata, read-time triple verification via a 'PrecisionSafeLoader', a startup Decimal-precision self-test, and a manual 5,000-chunk safety pause) built to generate two self-authored synthetic datasets at scale: a 10-billion-sample Pythagorean-triple corpus (via a closed-form 'Stifel' single-index generator) and a 20-billion-sample 'Universal Physics Pantheon' spanning 20 physical laws, sampled log-uniformly using 80-digit BigInt entropy and stored as exact Decimal values; (3) a 98-mode 'Hypothesis-Driven Negative Dataset' (HND) of hand-designed adversarial/near-miss perturbations (48 modes for the math domain, 50 for physics) meant to defeat statistical shortcut-learning; and (4) a proposed 'Zero-Bias Discovery' pipeline coupling a trained classifier's decision boundary (located via SciPy root-finding at P=0.5) with PySR symbolic regression to recover physical constants without being given the ground-truth continuous target.
threat_model: N/A (non-security paper). The paper's 'adversarial' and 'Chaos Mode' terminology refers to hand-crafted hard-negative perturbations used to stress-test numerical/physical generalization (e.g., off-by-one triples, dropped universal constants, unit-scale errors), not to an attacker, an attack surface, or a protected security property. There is no threat model in the AI-security sense.
limitations: (1) Single independent-author work, not peer-reviewed, published as a 'monograph'; no institutional affiliation or external validation is stated. (2) The paper's headline scale claims (10-billion mathematical samples, 20-billion physics samples, 30-billion total) describe the data-generation/integrity engineering, but the actual empirical extrapolation experiment establishing NumberNet's advantage is run on a small proof-of-concept (2,000 samples, 15 epochs, a smaller/mean-pooled architecture) rather than the full-scale production model -- this is a claims-vs-demonstrated-results gap that a reader must not conflate. (3) The Zero-Bias Discovery / constant-rediscovery pipeline (recovering G or other constants via PySR on a trained classifier's decision boundary) is described as configured but not yet executed; no rediscovered constant or symbolic-regression result is reported. (4) All datasets are self-generated synthetic data (Pythagorean triples and formulaic physics laws with known closed-form ground truth); there is no evaluation against real experimental data, external benchmarks, or independently published baselines. (5) The author documents his own implementation discrepancy: the config file specifies num_leaves=63 for LightGBM but the training script hardcodes num_leaves=31 at runtime. (6) The code/GitHub repository is not yet public ('will be made public after final submission'), so the described integrity pipeline and results are not independently reproducible at this time. (7) Section 11's claims about how GPT-4/Claude tokenize numbers via BPE and would benefit from a 'Math Coprocessor Routing Layer' are speculative extrapolation, not something tested in this paper.

datasets_used: Self-generated: 10-billion-sample primitive Pythagorean-triple corpus (Stifel single-index closed-form generator: a_n=2n+1, b_n=2n(n+1), c_n=2n(n+1)+1), with a 48-mode Hypothesis-Driven Negative Dataset (weighted 95% UltraHard/Near-Miss, 5% simpler perturbations)

Self-generated: 20-billion-sample 'Universal Physics Pantheon' spanning 20 physical laws (e.g., Relativity, Gravitation, Coulomb's Law, Stefan-Boltzmann, Ideal Gas, Lorentz Force, Schrodinger Kinetic, Schwarzschild Radius, Kepler's Third Law), sampled log-uniformly via 80-digit BigInt entropy, paired with a 50-mode physics Chaos Matrix
Small demonstration set for the cosmic-extrapolation experiment: 2,000 samples (1,000 Stifel triples + 1,000 off-by-one negatives), trained on n in [1,1000] and tested at n=10^50
quantitative_results: All three models (A, B, C) converge to roughly 99.98% accuracy on local-scale training data (n in [1,1000])
Models A (LightGBM + hand-engineered features) and B (LightGBM, raw inputs, non-deterministic) begin failing to distinguish Class-A (precision) Chaos Modes near the Float Wall at ~10^15
Models A and B collapse to 50.00% accuracy (equivalent to random guessing) on test inputs beyond ~10^20 magnitude

Float64 loses the ability to distinguish integers c and c+1 at n=10^16 (formally demonstrated via prove_float_collapse.py), which the paper argues is a mathematical/representational guarantee rather than a training deficiency

No quantitative accuracy figure is reported in the extracted text for NumberNet (Model C) specifically at 10^50 scale; its success there is asserted architecturally, not tabulated as a measured number alongside the Model A/B figures
baselines_compared: Model A: LightGBM (GBDT) given raw inputs plus 5 hand-engineered features including a feature that is effectively the label itself (is_perfect_match)
Model B: LightGBM (GBDT) given only raw variables, run non-deterministically, with BigInt inputs cast to float64 -- a deliberate stand-in for a naive practitioner pipeline

PySR 'Baseline (Ground Truth)' condition: the traditional AI Feynman-style symbolic regression setup (cited from Udrescu & Tegmark, not this paper's contribution), used only as an upper-bound reference for the not-yet-executed Zero-Bias Discovery pipeline
